reasoning: Latin 'ito' (go) follows the pattern of -o endings in Latin corresponding to -u endings in Umbrian, as seen in multiple examples like sestu→sisto
Latin 'frictum' (roasted) would follow the -um→-u pattern. The 'c' in Latin might correspond to 'h' in Umbrian (as in Sahta→Sanctam), giving frehtu
Latin 'pro re' (for the business) follows the pattern where 'pro' compounds appear in Umbrian, and 're' would likely remain similar, giving 'pure'
Latin 'tauros' (bulls) would follow the pattern of -os→-u ending in Umbrian, with the stem largely preserved as 'tauru'
Latin 'forum' (town square) follows the -um→-u pattern consistently seen in Umbrian, giving 'furu'
Latin 'inito' (go in) combines the prefix 'in-' (which becomes 'en-' in Umbrian with vowel harmony) with the verb 'ito'→'itu', giving 'enitu'
